ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated masala chai / chai tea term; 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03301103) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN0KN covers tea infusions broadly; no dedicated spiced-tea or masala-chai code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 represents tea as a substance; no specific masala chai concept exists in the published hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of chai tea (approximately 240 ml / 240 g) as typically served at a café or prepared at home.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, hot, prepared from tap water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 14355)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Chai tea (masala chai without milk) is nutritionally very close to plain brewed black tea per 100 g liquid. USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed black tea (FDC ID 171917) is used as the primary nutrient anchor. Spice additions at typical infusion concentrations contribute negligible macronutrients to the liquid. The Starbucks-served version described in the input is noted as 'no added sugar' per HPB Singapore endorsement, consistent with a plain spiced-tea profile. Values reflect the brewed liquid only.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson et al.; plain brewed tea analogue
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for plain spiced chai tea (no milk, no sugar). Plain brewed black tea contains negligible fermentable carbohydrates and has an effectively zero glycemic response. By analogy with plain tea and other near-zero-carbohydrate hot beverages documented in the University of Sydney GI Database, GI is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category to 'low'. Glycemic load per 240 g serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g per serving is below 0.8 g. This convention is documented per Project Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~7 mg per 240 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Plain chai tea without added milk or sugar contains approximately 7 mg sodium per cup, making it a hypertension-friendly beverage; individuals should avoid adding salt or high-sodium syrups.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate and effectively zero glycemic load when consumed without added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chai tea consumed without added sugar or sweetened syrups has a glycemic load of approximately zero per serving and is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; cinnamon and other spices may confer modest insulin-sensitising effects per preliminary evidence, though clinical reliance on this is not recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of polyphenolic antioxidants from black tea and spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain chai tea is free of saturated fat and cholesterol and provides flavonoids and polyphenols from black tea and spices such as cinnamon and cardamom, which are associated with modest cardioprotective effects in observational studies; benefits are negated if prepared with full-fat milk and added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chai tea, also known as masala chai, is a traditional South Asian hot beverage made by brewing black tea with a blend of aromatic spices including cinnamon, cardamom, ginger, cloves, and black pepper. The word 'chai' itself means 'tea' in Hindi and several other South Asian languages, so 'chai tea' is a Western-market term for the spiced variety. The beverage originates from the Indian subcontinent and has become globally popular. When prepared without milk or added sugar — as in the Starbucks Singapore version endorsed by the Health Promotion Board under its 'Healthier Choice' no-added-sugar programme — chai tea is an extremely low-calorie drink providing approximately 1 kcal per 100 ml, negligible protein and fat, and only trace carbohydrates. Its glycemic load per serving is effectively zero. The spice blend contributes polyphenolic antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds. Chai tea is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and individuals following halal or gluten-free diets. It is clinically appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or full-fat dairy. The flavour profile is warm, aromatic, mildly spicy, and slightly astringent from the tannins in black tea.
common_user_queries: Is chai tea good for diabetics?
How many calories in chai tea?
What spices are in chai tea?
Is chai tea suitable for hypertension?
What is the glycemic index of chai tea?

confidence_reason: Chai tea as a plain spiced black tea infusion without milk or sugar is nutritionally well-characterised by USDA SR Legacy brewed black tea data, which is an approved primary source. The adaptation is minor and defensible. GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain tea, as no direct GI measurement for masala chai exists in the published database. Confidence is therefore 'medium' rather than 'high'.
notes: 1. Nutrient values reflect the brewed liquid only (no milk, no added sugar), consistent with the HPB Singapore 'no added sugar' endorsement noted in the input description. 2. protein_g is naturally zero for plain brewed tea as this beverage contains no protein-contributing ingredients at infusion concentrations. fat_g is naturally zero by the nature of a water-based tea infusion. sugar_g is confirmed zero per the no-added-sugar formulation and USDA SR Legacy data. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as the tea leaves are not consumed. 3. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention per Project Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are 0.0 (confirmed zero) per USDA SR Legacy for brewed black tea. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg, calcium_mg, cholesterol_mg are 0.0 (confirmed zero or below detection threshold — trace) per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171917 for brewed black tea. 6. The Starbucks One Fullerton listing is noted as the commercial source; nutrient values are derived from the approved USDA source rather than the field dataing. 7. Multilingual names are provided for Singaporean context given the food is sold in Singapore; the dish originates from India and Hindi/Tamil names reflect the South Asian origin. 8. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301103 and FoodEx2 code AN0KN are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code for masala chai / chai tea exists in the published catalogues as of the accessed date.
